title: REST API pluralization inconsistency breaks client expectations
body:
Mixing singular and plural resource names (`/user/1` vs `/products/1`) confuses API consumers and makes auto-generated client SDKs inconsistent.

```
# BAD — inconsistent pluralization
GET /user/1          # singular
GET /products/1      # plural
GET /category/1      # singular
POST /orders         # plural

# GOOD — always plural
GET /users/1
GET /products/1
GET /categories/1
POST /orders

# Nested resources — plural all the way
GET /users/1/orders          # User's orders
GET /users/1/orders/42       # Specific order
GET /users/1/orders/42/items # Order items
```

Always use plural nouns for resource names. Use nouns, not verbs (`/users` not `/getUsers`). The HTTP method conveys the action: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (update), DELETE (remove).

title: Using POST for everything instead of proper HTTP methods loses semantics
body:
APIs that use POST for all operations lose the benefits of HTTP semantics: caching, idempotency guarantees, and browser/proxy behavior.

```
# BAD — POST for everything
POST /api/getUser     { "id": 1 }
POST /api/updateUser  { "id": 1, "name": "John" }
POST /api/deleteUser  { "id": 1 }

# GOOD — proper HTTP methods
GET    /api/users/1                        # Read (cacheable, safe)
PUT    /api/users/1  { "name": "John" }    # Replace (idempotent)
PATCH  /api/users/1  { "name": "John" }    # Partial update
DELETE /api/users/1                        # Delete (idempotent)
POST   /api/users    { "name": "John" }    # Create (not idempotent)
```

**Why it matters:**
- GET requests are cacheable by browsers and CDNs
- PUT and DELETE are idempotent — retrying is safe
- POST is not idempotent — retrying may create duplicates
- Proxies can prefetch GET requests for performance
- API documentation tools (Swagger) use methods for structure

The only time POST-for-everything makes sense is for GraphQL (single endpoint) or when you genuinely need to hide parameters from URL logs.

title: Returning 200 OK for errors hides failures from monitoring and clients
GraphQL N+1 query problem silently destroys database performance
Offset pagination becomes catastrophically slow on large datasets
API versioning in the URL path couples version to every client integration
Missing idempotency keys cause duplicate charges and orders on retry
GraphQL queries without depth limiting enable denial of service
PATCH vs PUT confusion leads to accidental data loss
Rate limit headers must include Retry-After or clients can't back off properly
GraphQL subscriptions over WebSocket need authentication on connection init
Exposing database IDs in API responses enables enumeration attacks
GraphQL resolver errors expose internal stack traces to API consumers
REST error responses without a consistent format break client error handling
GraphQL over-fetching at the schema level defeats its purpose
body:
Designing GraphQL types that mirror database tables with all columns makes over-fetching unavoidable — the client can only request what the schema exposes.

```graphql
# BAD — exposing everything, no thought given to the graph
type User {
  id: ID!
  name: String!
  email: String!
  passwordHash: String!  # SECURITY: Never expose!
  internalNotes: String! # BUSINESS: Clients shouldn't see
  createdAt: DateTime!
  updatedAt: DateTime!
  deletedAt: DateTime    # IMPLEMENTATION: Leaks soft-delete
}

# GOOD — intentional, client-focused schema
type User {
  id: ID!
  name: String!
  email: String!
  avatar: String
  posts(first: Int = 10, after: String): PostConnection!
  isFollowedByMe: Boolean! # Derived field, useful for UI
}

# Separate admin type with additional fields
type AdminUser {
  id: ID!
  name: String!
  email: String!
  role: Role!
  lastLoginAt: DateTime
  createdAt: DateTime!
}
```

Design GraphQL schemas for the consumer, not the database. Use separate types for different access levels (public User vs AdminUser). Add computed/derived fields that serve UI needs (`isFollowedByMe`, `totalPosts`) to reduce client-side computation.

title: HATEOAS links are rarely worth the implementation cost for modern APIs
body:
HATEOAS (Hypertext As The Engine Of Application State) adds navigational links to API responses. In practice, most API consumers ignore them.

```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "John",
  "_links": {
    "self": { "href": "/api/users/1" },
    "posts": { "href": "/api/users/1/posts" },
    "avatar": { "href": "/api/users/1/avatar" },
    "update": { "href": "/api/users/1", "method": "PATCH" },
    "delete": { "href": "/api/users/1", "method": "DELETE" }
  }
}
```

**When HATEOAS actually helps:**
- Pagination: `{ next: "/api/posts?cursor=abc", prev: "/api/posts?cursor=xyz" }` — clients use these directly
- File uploads: return the URL to access the uploaded file
- Workflow steps: guide the client through multi-step processes

**When to skip it:**
- SPAs and mobile apps that hardcode API paths in the client
- Internal microservice APIs
- APIs with typed client SDKs

If you want discoverability without full HATEOAS, use OpenAPI/Swagger documentation and generated client SDKs. They provide better DX than runtime link discovery.

title: GraphQL federation requires careful schema ownership boundaries
body:
In Apollo Federation, if two subgraphs try to extend the same type without clear ownership, you get confusing merge conflicts and runtime errors.

```graphql
# users-subgraph
type User @key(fields: "id") {
  id: ID!
  name: String!
  email: String!
}

# orders-subgraph — EXTENDS User with order data
type User @key(fields: "id") {
  id: ID!
  orders: [Order!]!
  totalSpent: Float!
}

# BAD — both subgraphs define 'name' on User
# orders-subgraph accidentally adds:
type User @key(fields: "id") {
  id: ID!
  name: String!       # CONFLICT! Already defined in users-subgraph
  orders: [Order!]!
}
```

**Best practices for federation:**
```graphql
# Each subgraph owns specific fields
# Users subgraph: owns User core fields
# Orders subgraph: extends User with order-related fields
# Reviews subgraph: extends User with review-related fields

# Use @external to reference fields from other subgraphs
type User @key(fields: "id") {
  id: ID!
  name: String! @external  # Defined elsewhere, just referencing
  orders: [Order!]! @requires(fields: "name") # Needs name for order confirmation
}
```

Create a schema registry and enforce ownership rules in CI. Each subgraph should only add fields it owns. Use `@external` when you need to reference another subgraph's fields for computed values.

title: OpenAPI/Swagger documentation drifts from actual API behavior without generation
Returning 404 for authorization failures leaks resource existence
GraphQL mutations should return the modified object, not just success/failure
Forgetting to set Content-Type header causes 415 or empty body parsing
API filtering via query params allows SQL injection when building queries dynamically
GraphQL persisted queries are required for production — not optional
Cursor pagination cursors must be opaque — not plaintext IDs
DELETE endpoints should be idempotent — return 200/204 even if resource already deleted
GraphQL union types require __typename for client-side type narrowing
Large JSON response payloads must be paginated or streamed — not sent all at once
REST API resource nesting beyond 2 levels creates brittle, hard-to-maintain URLs
GraphQL input types must be separate from output types
413 Payload Too Large — request body exceeds server limit
405 Method Not Allowed — route exists but HTTP method is wrong
GraphQL Error: Cannot return null for non-nullable field — schema nullability mismatch
ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT — sending response after headers already sent
GraphQL Error: Unknown type 'JSON' — missing scalar type definition
CORS error on DELETE/PUT/PATCH — missing CORS methods configuration
body:
CORS preflight checks fail for non-simple methods (PUT, PATCH, DELETE) when the server only allows GET and POST.

```
Access to fetch has been blocked by CORS policy: Method PATCH is not allowed
by Access-Control-Allow-Methods in preflight response.
```

```typescript
// BAD — default cors() may not include all methods
app.use(cors()); // Allows: GET, HEAD, POST (simple methods)

// Client:
fetch('/api/users/1', { method: 'PATCH', ... }); // Blocked!

// GOOD — explicitly allow needed methods
app.use(cors({
  origin: 'https://myapp.com',
  methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE', 'OPTIONS'],
  allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization', 'X-Request-ID'],
  exposedHeaders: ['X-Total-Count', 'X-Request-ID'], // Headers client can read
  credentials: true,
  maxAge: 86400, // Cache preflight for 24 hours
}));
```

`exposedHeaders` is often forgotten — by default, clients can only read `Cache-Control`, `Content-Language`, `Content-Type`, `Expires`, `Last-Modified`, and `Pragma` from cross-origin responses. Custom headers like `X-Total-Count` (for pagination) need to be explicitly exposed.

title: SyntaxError: Unexpected token < in JSON at position 0 — API returning HTML
GraphQL DataLoader batch function returned wrong number of results
TypeError: Cannot read property 'json' of undefined — missing async in route handler
GraphQL Error: Maximum call stack size exceeded — circular type references
409 Conflict — concurrent updates with optimistic locking
422 Unprocessable Entity vs 400 Bad Request — when to use which
GraphQL subscription memory leak — subscriptions not cleaned up on disconnect
504 Gateway Timeout — long-running API operations need async patterns
OpenAPI validation error: 'schema' must be an object — wrong YAML indentation
body:
OpenAPI YAML specs are extremely sensitive to indentation. A single misaligned field creates confusing validation errors.

```yaml
# BAD — wrong indentation
paths:
  /users:
    get:
      responses:
        '200':
          description: OK
          content:
            application/json:
            schema:                    # Wrong! 'schema' should be under 'application/json'
              type: array
              items:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/User'

# GOOD — correct indentation
paths:
  /users:
    get:
      responses:
        '200':
          description: OK
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:                  # Properly nested under content type
                type: array
                items:
                  $ref: '#/components/schemas/User'
```

Use an OpenAPI-aware editor (VS Code with OpenAPI extension, Swagger Editor) for real-time validation. Better yet, use code-first approaches (zod-to-openapi, tsoa) that generate valid YAML/JSON automatically.

REST vs GraphQL vs tRPC — choosing the right API paradigm
Cursor vs offset pagination — performance, UX, and implementation trade-offs
body:
Two pagination strategies with fundamentally different performance characteristics.

```typescript
// Offset pagination
GET /api/posts?page=50&limit=20
// SQL: SELECT * FROM posts ORDER BY created_at DESC OFFSET 980 LIMIT 20
// Problem: DB must scan 1000 rows to return 20

// Cursor pagination
GET /api/posts?cursor=eyJpZCI6MTAwMH0&limit=20
// SQL: SELECT * FROM posts WHERE created_at < '2024-01-15' ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20
// Always scans exactly 20 rows via index seek
```

| Feature | Offset | Cursor |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Performance | O(offset + limit) | O(limit) |
| Jump to page | Yes | No |
| Total count | Easy (COUNT(*)) | Expensive |
| Consistent | No (insertions shift pages) | Yes (stable position) |
| URL shareable | Yes (/posts?page=5) | Depends |
| Implementation | Simple | More complex |
| Duplicate/skip items | Yes (on insert/delete) | No |
| Best for | Admin panels, small datasets | Feeds, mobile, large datasets |

**Consistency problem with offset:**
```
# User viewing page 2 (items 11-20)
# Another user inserts an item at position 5
# Items shift: what was item 11 is now item 12
# When user loads page 3, they see item 20 again (it shifted from page 2 to page 3)
```

**Use offset when:** total count is small (<10K rows), users need random page access, you're building admin dashboards. **Use cursor when:** data changes frequently, datasets are large, building infinite scroll or feeds, consistency matters (no duplicate/missed items).
